content.full_text:
Marjorie Fernandes
1010 Noble Avenue
San Jose, CA 95132

MAR 31 11 US AP '92

March 25, 1992


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Commission Staff member,

During the year 1991, I was a Policy Director for the Mayor of San Jose. In filling out
my Form 730 as an annual statement, I had a question about how to handle two fees
that I received for the speeches noted below:

1. Keynote speech, Honors Luncheon San Jose State History Department (4/17)

2. Graduation speech -- Metropolitan Adult Education Program (6/11)

It was stated to me over the phone that I did not need to report the fees on the 730
Form for the following reasons:

• The fee for each speech was only $100
• Each speech required at least 8 hours of preparation
• My wages as a teacher and a mayorai aide were about $25 hour
• Schedule F states: "Gift means anything of value, whether tangible or
intangible, for which equal or greater value is not provided."
• Therefore, the fees I received were not gifts, but rather income

• Under Schedule D, only "Income aggregating $250 or more received from
any source located in or doing business in your jurisdiction must be
disclosed."

We concluded that the fees are not reportable on either Schedule D or Schedule F.
Please confirm this interpretation in writing to me at your earliest convenience.

Sincerely,

Marjorie Fernandes
408 926-6284


If there is text present, it is too faint or low contrast for me to read accurately according to your transcription rules. I cannot transcribe what I cannot clearly read.

sections.question: During the year 1991, I was a Policy Director for the Mayor of San Jose. In filling out my Form 730 as an annual statement, I had a question about how to handle two fees that I received for the speeches noted below: Keynote speech, Honors Luncheon San Jose State History Department (4/17) and Graduation speech -- Metropolitan Adult Education Program (6/1 1). It was stated to me over the phone that I did not need to report the fees on the 730 Form for the following reasons: The fee for each speech was only $100; Each speech required at least 8 hours of preparation; My wages as a teacher and a mayoral aide were about $25 hour; Schedule F states: 'Gift means anything of value, whether tangible or intangible, for which equal or greater value is not provided'; Therefore, the fees I received were not gifts, but rather income; Under Schedule D, only 'Income aggregating $250 or more received from any source located in or doing business in your jurisdiction must be disclosed.' We concluded that the fees are not reportable on either Schedule D or Schedule F. Please confirm this interpretation in writing to me at your earliest convenience.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks written confirmation that two $100 speaking fees received while serving as Policy Director for the Mayor of San Jose do not require reporting on Form 730 Schedule D or F, based on the reasoning that the fees constitute income rather than gifts and fall below the $250 disclosure threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice section present. The document contains a detailed question section explaining the requester's analysis and request for confirmation. Minor OCR issues noted in address and some text formatting, but core content is clear.

embedding.summary: Marjorie Fernandes, a 1991 Policy Director for the Mayor of San Jose, requests written confirmation from the FPPC that two $100 speaking fees she received need not be reported on her Form 730, arguing they constitute reportable income rather than gifts and fall below disclosure thresholds.
